name: The conquest of Makkah, part 5
epithet: The day the city he was driven from came to his hand

era.anchor: 8 AH, the days after the city opened

title: The conquest of Makkah, part 5: the adhan from the Ka'bah and the door that closed
meta_description: Day 80 of the seerah: Bilal gives the adhan from atop the Ka'bah, Makkah pours onto Safa to pledge, the women's bay'ah and Hind, and the holdouts who finally came in. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's seerah.
keywords: seerah, conquest of Makkah, fath Makkah, Bilal adhan Kaaba, bayah on Safa, no hijra after the conquest, Hind bint Utbah, Safwan ibn Umayyah, Suhayl ibn Amr, Abu Quhafah, Yasir Qadhi seerah

video.title: Seerah of Prophet Muhammad 80 - The Conquest of Makkah Part 5 ~ Dr. Yasir Qadhi | 26th March 2014

source_note: Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 80: the conquest of Makkah, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Makkah has opened. The man this city once drove into the desert is standing at the doors of the Ka'bah with those doors thrown open behind him, and the people who hunted him are gathered in front of him, waiting to learn what a conqueror does with the ones who wronged him. He tells them: go forth, you are free. And then, instead of a reckoning, the rest of the day fills up with something gentler and stranger: a voice on the roof of the Ka'bah, a line of hands on a hill, and a slow trickle of old enemies finding their way back, one heart at a time.
This is the fifth of our days inside the conquest, and it is the day the doors of Allah stand open and the only question left in Makkah is who will walk through them.

paras: Picture the scene, because the symbolism is too obvious to miss. The Prophet ﷺ stands at the open doors of the Ka'bah, the house at the center of the world he was born into and exiled from, and around him press the people of Makkah: the families of those who tortured his companions, the sons of those who plotted his death, the woman who had mutilated his uncle. By every law their century knew, this was the hour of revenge.

He gives them a single sentence instead: go forth, you are free. The doors of the house are open, and his invitation is open with them, anyone who wishes may enter Islam. Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers here on the picture itself, the doors of Allah flung wide and the Messenger ﷺ at the threshold calling a beaten city in, because mercy at the height of power is the whole lesson of the day. Strictness was the rare exception, shown only so the line would not be crossed. Mercy was the rule, and the rule was winning.

Then he gave one of the most famous instructions of his life. He told Bilal radiyallahu anhu to climb to the top of the Ka'bah and call the adhan from the highest point in Makkah. Even someone who knows almost nothing of this religion knows this much: that Allah honored Bilal to be the first human being ever to call the adhan over the holiest of houses.

Sit with what that voice had been through. It was the voice that cried out the oneness of Allah in the valleys of Makkah while his tormentors crushed him under the noon sun for saying it. It refused to break. And so it was that exact voice, the Sheikh draws out, that Allah chose to lift, literally and figuratively, above the Quraysh who had once stood over it. An Abyssinian with no lineage, no clan, no claim on this city, sent up onto its summit to proclaim the testimony of faith on behalf of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. The one who held firm through humiliation is the one raised at the hour of honor. As they were dealt with, so they were repaid: good for good.

Below him the elite of Quraysh listened. Attab ibn Asid, the full brother of Abu Jahl, said he thanked God his father was already dead and did not have to see this black man chosen above them. Al-Harith ibn Hisham said that if he truly believed this man was upon the truth, he would follow him. And Abu Sufyan, a Muslim now for only a few hours, held his tongue and said: I will say nothing, for if I did, these very stones would tell him what I said. He had come to believe the Prophet ﷺ was a prophet, yet faith had not yet settled into his heart. When the Prophet ﷺ passed them later, he told each man, word for word, exactly what he had said while no one was listening. Al-Harith took the testimony on the spot.

sections.h2: The hill where it ended, where it began

ayah.arabic: وَرَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا
ayah.text: And you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes,

paras: From the Ka'bah he made his way to Safa and sat down. There he received the bay'ah, the oath of allegiance, from everyone willing to embrace Islam, and they came in throngs, lining up, crowding around him, hand after hand after hand. This is that promised ayah made visible in front of you: the people entering the religion of Allah in multitudes.

And the place could not have been more perfect. Safa is where the public call began, nearly twenty years earlier, the day he ﷺ first climbed it to warn his clans. Now the whole circle closes on the same stone. Where the da'wah started, there it is sealed, and the man who once stood up on that hill to a hostile city now sits on it to receive that city home. The bulk of Makkah accepted Islam in this single stretch.

sections.h2: The door that closed behind them
paras: One exchange from that hill became a ruling for the whole ummah. A companion brought his own blood brother, freshly arrived, and asked the Prophet ﷺ to grant him the reward of the hijra, the migration, the way the early emigrants had earned it. It is one of the most famous things said during the conquest, recorded in Bukhari and Muslim: there is no hijra after the conquest of Makkah.

For eight years the great hijra to Madinah had been an obligation on every believer, your Islam was deficient without it. The day Makkah opened, that door shut. The Sheikh is careful about what this does and does not mean: the one and only Hijra, the migration that defined a generation, was now complete and could not be joined late. Personal migrations would still happen, a believer fleeing persecution for the safety of his faith, but the great communal Hijra was finished. What remained, the Prophet ﷺ said, was jihad and good intention. If the brother truly longed for that rank, the road ahead, against Rome and Persia, and a sincere heart, would still carry him there.

sections.h2: The women's pledge, and Hind behind the veil

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا جَاءَكَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتُ يُبَايِعْنَكَ عَلَىٰ أَن لَّا يُشْرِكْنَ بِاللَّهِ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَسْرِقْنَ وَلَا يَزْنِينَ وَلَا يَقْتُلْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ وَلَا يَأْتِينَ بِبُهْتَانٍ يَفْتَرِينَهُ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ وَلَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِي مَعْرُوفٍ ۙ فَبَايِعْهُنَّ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُنَّ اللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
ayah.text: O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging to you that they will not associate anything with Allah, nor will they steal, nor will they commit unlawful sexual intercourse, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their arms and legs, nor will they disobey you in what is right, then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

paras: When the men had finished, the women of Quraysh were called, and he took their pledge upon the very conditions Allah lays out in this ayah. Among them, hidden behind a full face veil, stood Hind bint Utbah, the wife of Abu Sufyan, the woman who had done to the body of Hamza radiyallahu anhu what every listener knows and dreads to repeat. She had reason to fear, and reason to hide.

But Hind could not hold her tongue, and her tongue gave her away. When he said do not associate anything with Allah, she muttered that he was asking of them what he had not asked the men, an assumption that was simply wrong. When he said do not steal, she shot back that she used to take from Abu Sufyan's wealth without his knowing, and Abu Sufyan, in the crowd, hurried to forgive whatever was past. The Prophet ﷺ said: are you Hind? She admitted it and begged pardon, and he did not punish her, and he did not say you are forgiven. He simply moved on. The Sheikh stops you on how perfect that was: it is easier to forgive a wrong done to yourself than one done to someone you love, and harder still when it was done to the dead. He neither struck back nor pretended the wound was nothing. He let it go and let her go.

When he said do not commit unlawful relations, she snapped, does a free woman do such a thing? And when he said do not kill your children, came the line that undid the room: as for that, we raised them as little ones and you killed them as grown men at Badr. It was so sharp, so unexpected, that one of the great companions sitting behind the Prophet ﷺ burst out laughing and fell onto his back. And it is recorded that the Prophet ﷺ never took a woman's hand in pledge; his word to them was the pledge itself.

Quietly, the Ansar of Madinah began to ache. They watched him return to the city of his birth, watched his kin and his cousins and the Quraysh come pouring into Islam, and a thought went around among them: now that he is home, softness for his own people has taken hold of him, he will forget us. At the root of it was not jealousy so much as love, the fear of a beloved drifting back to where he came from.

Jibril brought him their words, yet the first thing he did was ask them, did you say this? out of pure courtesy, to give them the chance to speak, even though he already knew. They confessed. And then he reminded them who he was: had he not, years ago at Aqabah, made them a promise that his life was their life and his death their death? Who did they think he was, that he would break it now? He was theirs, he told them, in living and in dying, gathered into them and no longer merely a man of his own clan. The Ansar wept, and asked forgiveness, and he forgave them, and Allah and His Messenger believed their excuse.

sections.h2: The ones who came in slowly

ayah.arabic: قَالَتِ الْأَعْرَابُ آمَنَّا ۖ قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَلَٰكِن قُولُوا أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمَانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ لَا يَلِتْكُم مِّنْ أَعْمَالِكُمْ شَيْئًا ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
ayah.text: The bedouins say, "We have believed." Say, "You have not [yet] believed; but say [instead], 'We have submitted,' for faith has not yet entered your hearts. And if you obey Allah and His Messenger, He will not deprive you from your deeds of anything. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."

paras: The Prophet ﷺ stayed in Makkah for about three weeks, and the holdouts came in over those days, one reluctant heart at a time. Islam, the Sheikh reminds you, arrives in levels: Allah Himself tells the bedouins not to claim belief when they have only just submitted, for faith had not yet entered their hearts. He saw it then and we see it now, that for someone not raised inside this faith, surrender can take time, and our part is to be the more patient and the more generous.

There was Fadala, a young man so enraged by the defeat that he hid a dagger and crept up behind the Prophet ﷺ during his tawaf, meaning to kill him and not caring that he would die for it. The Prophet ﷺ turned: is this Fadala? What were you intending? Nothing, he lied, I was only remembering Allah. The Prophet ﷺ laughed at the open lie, laid his hand on Fadala's chest, and in that instant, Fadala later said, no one on earth was more beloved to him than the man before him. The plot dissolved into faith.

There was Safwan ibn Umayyah, who fled toward the sea to drown himself rather than live under this, until his cousin caught him at the shore carrying the Prophet's own turban as a token of safety, and brought him back. The Prophet ﷺ gave him four months to decide, asked nothing of his heart, and later, after Hunayn, gave him a whole valley of spoils until Safwan said only a prophet could give like this, and his hatred turned to love. There was Suhayl ibn Amr, the proud negotiator of Hudaybiyyah, who locked himself in his house in terror and sent his son to beg protection, and was told to be received with respect and never stared at in anger, for he was a man of intelligence and honor who would come to Islam when he saw it. And there was Abu Quhafah, the aged blind father of Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu, led in by the hand, for whom the Prophet ﷺ said, you should have left the old man at home, we would have come to him, the dignity of the elder honored for the sake of his son. One by one, the city he had been driven from came home.

dua.translit: Allahumma anta as-salam wa minka as-salam, tabarakta ya dhal-jalali wal-ikram
dua.en: O Allah, You are Peace, and from You is peace. Blessed are You, O Owner of majesty and honor. And O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who entered his city in victory and answered it with mercy.

lessons_intro: The conquest is not a story about winning. It is a story about what a believer does the hour after he wins. These threads run straight out of the Sheikh's telling.

lessons.text: He held an entire city at his mercy and said: go forth, you are free. The measure of faith is not what you do when you are wronged, but what you do the moment you could finally get even.

lessons.bold: You are paid in your own coin.
lessons.text: The voice that cried tawheed under torture was the voice raised above the Ka'bah at the hour of honor. As you deal, so you are dealt with. Hold firm in the hard years and trust Allah with the repayment.
He forgave Hind without saying you are forgiven, and let her go. You can release a wound to Allah without rushing to declare it healed. Mercy and honesty about the hurt can sit in the same heart.
Some entered Islam and soared; some, like Abu Sufyan, vacillated for a while. Allah told the bedouins as much. Be patient and generous with hearts still on the road, including your own.

When the Ansar feared he would forget them, he reminded them his life was their life and his death their death. The bond he gave his people, he gives you: belong to him ﷺ and trust that he never forgets his own.

faq.q: Why is Bilal giving the adhan from the Ka'bah such a celebrated moment?
faq.a: Because the same voice that proclaimed the oneness of Allah while Bilal radiyallahu anhu was tortured in the valleys of Makkah was the voice Allah chose to raise above the holiest house on earth. An Abyssinian once enslaved and persecuted, with no lineage among Quraysh, was lifted literally and figuratively above the elite who had stood over him. Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws the principle from it: the one who held firm in humiliation is honored at the hour of victory, you are dealt with as you deserve.

faq.q: What does "there is no hijra after the conquest of Makkah" mean?
faq.a: For eight years the migration to Madinah was an obligation on every Muslim. When Makkah was conquered, that specific communal Hijra was complete and could no longer be joined, so its unique reward could not be earned late. It does not abolish all migration: a believer may still migrate for the safety of his faith. What the Prophet ﷺ said remained, in its place, was jihad and sincere intention. The hadith is in Bukhari and Muslim.

faq.q: How did the Prophet ﷺ respond to Hind bint Utbah?
faq.a: Hind, behind a face veil, kept interjecting through the women's pledge, and her words gave away her identity. After what she had done to Hamza radiyallahu anhu, the Prophet ﷺ neither punished her nor declared her forgiven, he simply moved on. The Sheikh notes the wisdom: it is harder to forgive a crime against a loved one, especially against the dead, and he neither retaliated nor pretended the wound did not exist. He let it go without forcing himself to say more.

faq.q: Why were the Ansar upset, and how did the Prophet ﷺ reassure them?
faq.a: Seeing him reunited with his Makkan kin, the Ansar feared he would settle back among his own people and forget Madinah. The root of it was love, not resentment. Though Jibril told him what they had said, he asked them first out of courtesy, then reminded them of his promise at Aqabah that his life was their life and his death their death. They wept and he accepted their excuse.

faq.q: Who were some of the people who accepted Islam only after the conquest?
faq.a: Over the roughly three weeks the Prophet ﷺ stayed in Makkah, several holdouts came in: Fadala, who abandoned an assassination attempt the instant the Prophet ﷺ laid a hand on his chest; Safwan ibn Umayyah, who fled to drown himself and was won back, then won over by overwhelming generosity after Hunayn; Suhayl ibn Amr, the negotiator of Hudaybiyyah; and Abu Quhafah, the elderly blind father of Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu. Their slow journeys show that faith, for many, takes time.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: The conquest is the hour every wronged person dreams of and almost no one survives with their soul intact: total power over the ones who hurt you. He ﷺ walked into it and walked out the other side gentler than he went in. He freed them. He raised the voice they had crushed. He sat on the hill they once chased him from and received them home. He forgave the woman who had desecrated his uncle's body by simply letting her pass. And for three patient weeks he waited, hand open, while the hardest hearts in Arabia found their way in one at a time. This is what victory looks like in the hands of a mercy to the worlds.

O Allah, You are Peace and from You is peace; let the heart that has been wronged answer with the mercy he answered with, and open for us the doors You opened for Makkah. Send Your praise and Your peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, the one his city drove out and then could not help but follow home, and gather us among those who come to him, however slowly, and are received. Ameen.

slides:
Day 80 of 104.

The city that drove him out is now at his mercy. The doors of the Ka'bah are open behind him.

He tells them: go forth, you are free.

He sends Bilal to the top of the Ka'bah to call the adhan.

The same voice that cried tawheed under torture in these valleys, now raised above the holiest house on earth.

As you deal, so you are dealt with.

Below, the elite mutter. Abu Sufyan, a Muslim for only hours, holds his tongue: if I speak, these stones will tell him.

Faith had reached his head. Not yet his heart.

Allah says: you see the people entering the religion of Allah in multitudes.

He sits on Safa, the hill where the call began twenty years ago, and the whole city lines up to pledge. The circle closes.

A man asks the reward of the great Hijra for his brother.

"There is no hijra after the conquest of Makkah."

The door that defined a generation had quietly closed. What remained was good intention.

Behind a veil stands Hind, who mutilated Hamza at Uhud.

He does not punish her. He does not say you are forgiven.

He simply moves on. Mercy and an honest wound, in one heart.

The Ansar fear he will forget them now that he is home.

He reminds them of his promise: my life is your life, my death your death.

They weep. He is theirs.

And the holdouts trickle in: Fadala, who came to kill him and left in love. Safwan, won by a valley of gifts. Suhayl. Old blind Abu Quhafah, led in by the hand.

One heart at a time, the city came home.
social.caption: Day 80 of 104: the conquest of Makkah, part 5. The man this city exiled now holds it at his mercy, and answers with the open door. Bilal's adhan from the Ka'bah, the city pledging on Safa, the close of the great Hijra, Hind behind the veil, and the enemies who came back one heart at a time. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's seerah series (episode embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A new day of his life ﷺ, every day, at buruja.com. #seerah #ProphetMuhammad #conquestofMakkah #Buruja #YasirQadhi #islam #sunnah #Bilal

email.subject: Go forth, you are free
email.alt_subject: The voice they crushed, raised above the Ka'bah
email.preview: Day 80 of 104: what the Prophet ﷺ did the hour after he won.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

The conquest is the hour almost no one survives with their soul intact: total power over the people who hurt you.

The man Makkah drove into the desert is now standing at the open doors of the Ka'bah, and the families who tortured his companions and plotted his death are gathered in front of him, waiting for the reckoning. He gives them one sentence: go forth, you are free.

Then, instead of revenge, the day fills with something gentler. He sends Bilal to the top of the Ka'bah to call the adhan, the same voice that cried out the oneness of Allah while he was crushed under the noon sun for saying it, now lifted above the very people who once stood over him. As you deal, so you are dealt with.

He sits on Safa, the hill where the public call began twenty years before, and the whole city lines up to pledge, the people entering the religion of Allah in throngs. He tells a companion there is no hijra after the conquest of Makkah, and a door that defined a generation quietly closes. He meets Hind, who had mutilated his uncle Hamza, and neither punishes her nor pretends the wound is nothing, he simply lets her pass. He reassures the Ansar, who feared he would forget them, that his life is their life and his death their death.

And then he waits, hand open, for three patient weeks while the hardest hearts in Arabia find their way in one at a time: the man who came to assassinate him and left in love, the one who fled to drown himself and was won back by sheer generosity, the proud negotiator, the old blind father led in by the hand.

This is what victory looks like in the hands of a mercy to the worlds. Read day 80, and watch the Sheikh's full episode, at the link below.
